content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 16 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 16 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 16:1 — "the collection for the saints" / "the collection, the [one] for the holy [ones]" / "the money being collected for God's people in Jerusalem"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the collection, the [one] for the holy [ones]" | Literal with bracketed article and "holy ones" for hagioi |
| LOGOS | "the collection for the saints" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the money being collected for God's people in Jerusalem" | Adds "money," "God's people," and "in Jerusalem" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's additions ("money," "in Jerusalem") are contextually accurate but add details Paul does not state in this verse. "Saints" (hagioi) is the standard Pauline term for believers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:1 — "the churches of Galatia" / "the assemblies of Galatia" / "the churches in Galatia"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the assemblies of Galatia" | Renders ekklesia as "assembly" |
| LOGOS | "the churches of Galatia" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the churches in Galatia" | "In" instead of "of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Consistent rendering of ekklesia as "churches."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:2 — "as he may prosper" / "whatever he may be prospered" / "a portion of the money you have earned"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "storing up whatever he may be prospered" | Literal hoti ean euodotai |
| LOGOS | "as he may prosper" | Concise formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "you should each put aside a portion of the money you have earned" | Full paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "as he may prosper." PNEUMA specifies "money" and "earned," which narrows the Greek (euodotai can include any form of prospering). EMET's passive "be prospered" is awkward in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:9 — "a wide door for effective work" / "a door, great and effective" / "a wide-open door for a great work"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a door, great and effective, has opened to me" | Literal word order |
| LOGOS | "a wide door for effective work has opened to me" | Interprets "great" as "wide" (applied to door) |
| PNEUMA | "There is a wide-open door for a great work here" | "Wide-open" and "great work" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Wide door" naturalizes the Greek megale ("great") when applied to a door. PNEUMA's "wide-open" is slightly redundant (an opened door is already open).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:10 — "put him at ease" / "he is with you without fear" / "don't intimidate him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "see that he is with you without fear" | Literal aphobos — "without fear" |
| LOGOS | "see that you put him at ease among you" | Dynamic interpretation |
| PNEUMA | "don't intimidate him" | Negative command form |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "put him at ease." EMET's "without fear" is literal. PNEUMA's "don't intimidate" reverses the construction from positive to negative and implies the Corinthians were already intimidating Timothy. LOGOS captures the sense without that implication.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:13 — "act like men" / "be courageous" / "Be courageous"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "be courageous" | Renders andrizesthe as "be courageous" |
| LOGOS | "act like men" | Literal andrizesthe — "be manly/act courageously" |
| PNEUMA | "Be courageous" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "act like men." The Greek andrizesthe is etymologically connected to aner ("man") and means "play the man/be courageous." While EMET and PNEUMA's "be courageous" captures the meaning, LOGOS preserves the etymological connection. The consensus retains this traditional rendering while noting in the council notes that the term functions as a general exhortation to courage.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Etymological rendering preserved.

---

## 16:14 — "Let all that you do be done in love" / "Let all the [things] of you be done in love" / "And do everything with love"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Let all the [things] of you be done in love" | Literal with bracketed article |
| LOGOS | "Let all that you do be done in love" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "And do everything with love" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. This is the summary verse of the entire letter. LOGOS's formal rendering preserves the weight of the imperative. PNEUMA's simplification, while clear, lacks the gravitas of the Greek construction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:15 — "first converts in Achaia" / "[the] firstfruit of Achaia" / "the first of the harvest of believers in Greece"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] firstfruit of Achaia" | Literal aparche — "firstfruit" |
| LOGOS | "the first converts in Achaia" | Interprets aparche as "first converts" |
| PNEUMA | "the first of the harvest of believers in Greece" | Adds "harvest of believers"; substitutes "Greece" for "Achaia" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "first converts." EMET's "firstfruit" preserves the agricultural metaphor but is less clear for modern readers. PNEUMA substitutes "Greece" for the Roman provincial name "Achaia," which changes the historical reference.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:19 — "Aquila and Prisca" / "Akulas and Priska" / "Aquila and Priscilla"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Akulas and Priska" | Transliterated forms |
| LOGOS | "Aquila and Prisca" | Paul's spelling (Priska, not Priskilla) |
| PNEUMA | "Aquila and Priscilla" | Familiar diminutive form (from Acts/later tradition) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Aquila and Prisca" (LOGOS). Paul consistently uses the formal name Prisca (not the diminutive Priscilla used in Acts). The consensus follows Paul's own usage.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:22 — "Our Lord, come!" / "Marana tha!" / "Our Lord, come!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Marana tha!" | Preserves untranslated Aramaic |
| LOGOS | "Our Lord, come!" | Translates the Aramaic |
| PNEUMA | "Our Lord, come!" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "Our Lord, come!" with the note that the underlying Aramaic is "Marana tha." The consensus text translates for accessibility while preserving the Aramaic in EMET's apparatus and SOPHIA's council notes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's Aramaic preserved in commentary.

---

## 16:22 — "has no love for the Lord" / "does not love the Lord" / "does not love the Lord"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If anyone does not love the Lord" | Literal ou philei |
| LOGOS | "If anyone has no love for the Lord" | Restructured with "has no love" |
| PNEUMA | "If anyone does not love the Lord" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "has no love for the Lord." Slightly more emphatic than a simple negation of the verb, capturing the weight of the anathema that follows.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 16:24 — inclusion/omission of "Amen"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | No "Amen" | Some manuscripts include, others omit |
| LOGOS | "Amen" | Includes the liturgical closing |
| PNEUMA | No "Amen" | Omits |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Amen" (LOGOS). Sinaiticus includes the Amen at the close of the letter. As the primary manuscript, its reading is followed.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA expansion/substitution | 5 | Added "money," "Jerusalem," "Greece" for "Achaia," "Priscilla" for "Prisca" |
| Name rendering | 3 | Prisca/Priscilla, Akulas/Aquila, Marana tha translation |
| EMET literalism | 4 | "Assembly," "firstfruit," untranslated Aramaic, bracketed articles |
| Terminology precision | 2 | "Act like men" etymology, "firstfruit" vs. "first converts" |
| Textual variant | 1 | Amen inclusion |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 16 is primarily practical (travel plans, greetings, instructions), and the disagreements are correspondingly less theologically weighty than in previous chapters. The most notable divergences involve name forms (Prisca vs. Priscilla, Marana tha), the rendering of aparche ("firstfruit"/"first converts"), and PNEUMA's tendency to substitute familiar terms for Paul's specific vocabulary (Greece for Achaia, Priscilla for Prisca). The consensus consistently preserved Paul's own terminology and the Sinaiticus readings.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
